text: He conquers twice who upon victory overcomes himself.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 68, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: He is powerful who governs himself.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 609, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Speech is the mirror of the soul; as a man speaks, so is he.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 1073, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: It is better to learn late than never.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 864, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Never promise more than you can perform.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 528, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Pardon one offense, and you encourage the commission of many.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 750, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Many receive advice; only the wise profit by it.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 149, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: In a heated argument we are apt to lose sight of the truth.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 20, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: It is a bad plan that admits of no modification.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 469, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Yield not to calamity, but face it boldly.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 982, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: He who does not prevent a wrong when he can does it himself.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 208, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: The miser is always poor.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 441, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Do not turn back when you are just at the goal.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 580, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Never find your delight in another's misfortune.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 288, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: A good reputation is more valuable than money.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 108, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Frugality is a great income in itself.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 236, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: A hasty judgment is a first step to recantation.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 257, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: No pleasure endures unseasoned by variety.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 406, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: It is not every question that deserves an answer.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 582, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Fortune is not satisfied with inflicting one calamity.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 274, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Anyone can hold the helm when the sea is calm.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 358, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Pain of mind is worse than pain of body.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 4, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Treat your friend as if he might one day be your enemy, and your enemy as if he might one day be your friend.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 4, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: A sword is never a joke.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 914, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: What is left when honor is lost?
source: Moral Sayings, No. 265, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: The fear of death is more to be dreaded than death itself.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 511, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Better to live in poverty with honor than in abundance with shame.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 119, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: He who helps in the saving of others saves himself as well.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 1050, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: No man is happy who does not think himself so.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 584, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: As men, we are all equal in the presence of death.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 1, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: You should go to a pear tree for pears, not to an elm.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 674, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Practice is the best of all instructors.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 439, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: A rolling stone can gather no moss.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 524, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: Where there is unity there is always victory.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 771, trans. D. Lyman (1856)

text: When fortune is on our side, popular favor bears her company.
source: Moral Sayings, No. 275, trans. D. Lyman (1856)
